title: Isaiah Chapter 35
subtitle: The Ransomed Return with Joy to Zion
structureSubtitle: See the desert bloom and the redeemed come home

verses.text: The desert and the parched land will be glad; the wilderness will rejoice and blossom. Like the crocus,

it will burst into bloom; it will rejoice greatly and shout for joy. The glory of Lebanon will be given to it, the splendor of Carmel and Sharon; they will see the glory of the LORD, the splendor of our God.
Strengthen the feeble hands, steady the knees that give way;
say to those with fearful hearts, "Be strong, do not fear; your God will come, he will come with vengeance; with divine retribution he will come to save you."
Then will the eyes of the blind be opened and the ears of the deaf unstopped.
Then will the lame leap like a deer, and the mute tongue shout for joy. Water will gush forth in the wilderness and streams in the desert.
The burning sand will become a pool, the thirsty ground bubbling springs. In the haunts where jackals once lay, grass and reeds and papyrus will grow.
And a highway will be there; it will be called the Way of Holiness; it will be for those who walk on that Way. The unclean will not journey on it; wicked fools will not go about on it.
No lion will be there, nor any ravenous beast; they will not be found there. But only the redeemed will walk there,
and those the LORD has rescued will return. They will enter Zion with singing; everlasting joy will crown their heads. Gladness and joy will overtake them, and sorrow and sighing will flee away.

1.seeing: The chapter opens with creation responding to God's redemptive work. The desert—symbol of desolation and judgment (chapter 34)—is now glad. The crocus blooming in the wilderness is an image of impossible beauty springing from barren ground.
1.life: Where is the desert in your life right now? This verse promises that barren seasons are not the end of the story. God specializes in making dead places bloom—not just survive, but rejoice and blossom.
1.teach: Creation itself participates in God's redemptive plan. Just as Romans 8 describes all creation groaning, here creation celebrates. The wilderness blooming signals that God's salvation touches everything—not just souls, but the whole created order.

2.seeing: Lebanon, Carmel, and Sharon were the most lush, fertile landscapes in Israel. Now their glory is given to the desert. The climax: 'they will see the glory of the LORD.' The blooming is not the point—seeing God's glory is.
2.life: Have you ever experienced God turning your worst season into your most beautiful? The desert doesn't just recover—it receives the glory of the finest places. God doesn't just restore; He lavishes abundance where there was nothing.
2.teach: The ultimate purpose of transformation is revelation—seeing God's glory and splendor. Physical restoration points to spiritual reality. When God redeems, He doesn't do halfway work; the desert receives Lebanon's glory because God's splendor demands extravagance.

3.seeing: The focus shifts from landscape to people. Feeble hands and weak knees represent those exhausted by suffering, exile, and waiting. The command is communal—strengthen one another, don't let the weary give up.
3.life: Who around you has feeble hands or weak knees today? This verse is a call to be the one who steadies others. Sometimes the most spiritual thing you can do is hold someone up when they're about to collapse.
3.teach: God's people have a responsibility to strengthen one another. Hebrews 12:12 quotes this verse directly, showing that encouragement is not optional but essential. The redeemed community is meant to carry one another toward the promise.

4.seeing: This is the hinge of the entire chapter. Every healing that follows flows from this announcement: 'Your God will come.' The fearful heart needs a word before it needs a miracle. God's coming includes both vengeance on oppressors and salvation for His people—two sides of one rescue.
4.life: What are you afraid of today? This verse speaks directly to anxious hearts: 'Be strong, do not fear.' Not because circumstances will improve on their own, but because your God will come. The basis for courage is God's arrival, not your strength.
4.teach: Divine vengeance and divine salvation are not contradictory—they are complementary. God's retribution falls on what oppresses His people, and His salvation lifts the oppressed. The command 'Be strong' is grounded in theology, not positive thinking: your God will come.

5.seeing: The healings begin immediately after the promise of God's coming. Blind eyes opening and deaf ears unstopping are both physical reversals and spiritual metaphors. These are signs of the messianic age—the very miracles Jesus performed.
5.life: What are you blind to that God wants you to see? What have you been deaf to that He's been saying? Physical healing pictures a deeper reality: God opening our perception to His truth, beauty, and presence.
5.teach: Jesus explicitly pointed to these healings as evidence of His messianic identity (Matthew 11:5). Isaiah 35:5 is not just poetry—it's a prophetic checklist. When the blind see and the deaf hear, the Messiah has arrived.

6.seeing: The lame don't just walk—they leap like a deer. The mute don't just whisper—they shout for joy. God's healing is extravagant, overflowing. And creation mirrors the healing: water gushes in the wilderness, streams appear in the desert.
6.life: God's restoration goes beyond fixing what's broken—it brings exuberant life. The lame person doesn't hobble; they leap. When God heals you, expect more than survival. Expect joy you couldn't have imagined.
6.teach: The parallel between human healing and creation's renewal shows they are part of one redemptive act. When God saves people, He also renews the earth. This points to the new creation where all things are made whole—bodies, hearts, and the ground itself.

7.seeing: The burning sand becoming a pool is a complete inversion of nature's harshness. Where jackals once roamed—signs of desolation and ruin—now grass, reeds, and papyrus grow. The transformation is total: predator territory becomes a garden.
7.life: Are there places in your life that feel like jackal territory—abandoned, ruined, dangerous? God promises that even those places can become lush and fruitful. His restoration doesn't skip the worst spots; it starts there.
7.teach: The reversal imagery—burning sand to pools, jackal haunts to gardens—demonstrates God's power over entropy and decay. What sin and judgment have ruined, redemption reclaims. This is not mere repair but re-creation, anticipating Revelation 21's 'I am making everything new.'

8.seeing: The Highway of Holiness is the central image of this section. It's a prepared road—safe, sacred, and exclusive to the redeemed. 'The unclean will not journey on it' means this path is set apart for those God has rescued. No wicked fool can stumble onto it.
8.life: God doesn't just save you—He gives you a path to walk on. The Highway of Holiness means you don't have to figure out the way alone. It's prepared, protected, and set apart. Your job is to walk on it, not build it.
8.teach: Ancient highways were royal roads built for safe travel. The Way of Holiness echoes Isaiah 40:3's 'prepare the way of the Lord.' This highway is both literal (return from exile) and eschatological (the path to God's eternal city). Jesus calls Himself 'the way' (John 14:6), fulfilling this image.

9.seeing: No lion, no ravenous beast—the dangers of ancient travel are removed. The highway is completely safe. 'Only the redeemed will walk there' emphasizes exclusivity: this road belongs to those God has purchased and rescued.
9.life: The fears that keep you from moving forward—the lions on your path—God promises to remove them. The Highway of Holiness is not a dangerous journey through hostile territory. It's a protected passage home for those God loves.
9.teach: The absence of predators contrasts sharply with chapter 34's wild animals inhabiting ruined Edom. What judgment fills with beasts, redemption clears for safe passage. The redeemed are defined not by their own merit but by God's act of redemption—they are His purchased people.

10.seeing: The chapter's climax: the ransomed return with singing. 'Everlasting joy will crown their heads' is not temporary happiness but permanent, unshakeable gladness. The final line is stunning: 'sorrow and sighing will flee away'—as if grief itself becomes the exile.
10.life: Every sorrow you carry has an expiration date. This verse promises that joy isn't just coming—it will overtake you, like someone running to catch up and embrace you. Sorrow and sighing don't just diminish; they flee. They become the ones in exile.
10.teach: This verse is quoted in Revelation 21:4 where God 'will wipe every tear from their eyes.' The ransomed of the LORD find their ultimate homecoming not just in returning to earthly Zion but in entering the new Jerusalem. Everlasting joy is the final state of the redeemed—not earned but crowned upon them by grace.

from: Psalm 96:11-12 - Let the fields be jubilant and everything in them; let all the trees of the forest sing for joy
Isaiah 41:18-19 - I will make rivers flow on barren heights and springs within the valleys
to: Romans 8:19-21 - The creation waits in eager expectation for the children of God to be revealed; creation itself will be liberated
Revelation 22:1-2 - The river of the water of life flows from the throne of God, and the tree of life yields fruit every month
1.context: Creation's joy at redemption is a theme running from the Psalms through Paul to Revelation. The desert blooming anticipates the day when all creation is set free from its bondage to decay and shares in the glory of God's children.

from: Isaiah 40:5 - The glory of the LORD will be revealed, and all people will see it together
Psalm 72:19 - May the whole earth be filled with His glory
to: John 1:14 - The Word became flesh and made His dwelling among us; we have seen His glory
2 Corinthians 3:18 - We all, who with unveiled faces contemplate the Lord's glory, are being transformed into His image
2.context: Seeing God's glory is the purpose of all creation's renewal. In Christ, God's glory became visible in human flesh. The desert's transformation points to a greater revelation: God's splendor seen face to face through Jesus.

from: Deuteronomy 31:6 - Be strong and courageous. Do not be afraid; the LORD your God goes with you
Isaiah 40:29-31 - He gives strength to the weary and increases the power of the weak
to: Hebrews 12:12 - Strengthen your feeble arms and weak knees
Galatians 6:2 - Carry each other's burdens, and in this way you will fulfill the law of Christ
3.context: The call to strengthen the weak is repeated across Scripture because God's people always include the weary. Hebrews directly quotes Isaiah 35:3, applying it to the church: mutual encouragement is essential for enduring faith.

from: Psalm 94:1-2 - The LORD is a God who avenges; O God who avenges, shine forth
Deuteronomy 32:35 - It is mine to avenge; I will repay. In due time their foot will slip
to: Matthew 11:2-5 - Jesus answered: the blind receive sight, the lame walk, the deaf hear—tell John what you see
Revelation 22:12 - Look, I am coming soon! My reward is with me, and I will give to each according to what they have done
4.context: God's coming brings both judgment and salvation simultaneously. When Jesus listed His miracles to John the Baptist, He was essentially quoting Isaiah 35:4-6, confirming that 'your God has come.' The vengeance and the healing are one rescue.

from: Isaiah 29:18 - In that day the deaf will hear the words of the scroll, and out of gloom and darkness the eyes of the blind will see
Psalm 146:8 - The LORD gives sight to the blind, the LORD lifts up those who are bowed down
to: Matthew 11:5 - The blind receive sight, the lame walk, the deaf hear—report to John what you hear and see
John 9:39 - Jesus said, 'For judgment I have come into this world, so that the blind will see and those who see will become blind'
5.context: Jesus' healing miracles were not random acts of compassion—they were fulfillment markers. Each blind eye opened and each deaf ear unstopped declared: the messianic age Isaiah promised has arrived. The physical healings point to spiritual awakening.

from: Isaiah 41:17-18 - The poor and needy search for water, but there is none; I the LORD will answer them, I will make rivers flow on barren heights
Joel 3:18 - In that day the mountains will drip new wine, and the hills will flow with milk; all the ravines will run with water
to: Acts 3:8 - He jumped to his feet and began to walk. Then he went with them into the temple courts, walking and jumping and praising God
John 7:38 - Whoever believes in me, rivers of living water will flow from within them
6.context: The lame man healed in Acts 3 literally fulfills the leaping of Isaiah 35:6. Peter and John's miracle was a living sign that the messianic age had dawned. Jesus' promise of living water flowing from believers extends the wilderness streams into spiritual reality.

from: Isaiah 34:13 - Thorns will overrun her citadels; jackals will inhabit her strongholds (Edom's judgment)
Isaiah 43:19-20 - I am making a way in the wilderness and streams in the wasteland; the wild animals honor me, the jackals and the owls
to: Revelation 21:5 - He who was seated on the throne said, 'I am making everything new!'
2 Peter 3:13 - In keeping with His promise we are looking forward to a new heaven and a new earth, where righteousness dwells
7.context: The contrast between chapters 34 and 35 is deliberate: judgment fills Edom with jackals, but redemption transforms jackal haunts into gardens. God's re-creation doesn't just restore Eden—it reclaims even the most desolate places. This finds ultimate fulfillment in the new heavens and new earth.

from: Isaiah 11:16 - There will be a highway for the remnant of His people, as there was for Israel when they came up from Egypt
Isaiah 40:3 - In the wilderness prepare the way for the LORD; make straight in the desert a highway for our God
to: John 14:6 - Jesus answered, 'I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me'
Hebrews 10:19-20 - We have confidence to enter the Most Holy Place by the blood of Jesus, by a new and living way
8.context: Isaiah's Highway of Holiness anticipates Jesus' declaration 'I am the way.' The ancient road for returning exiles becomes a person—Christ Himself is the sacred path to God. Hebrews calls it 'a new and living way,' opened through His sacrifice.

from: Psalm 107:2-3 - Let the redeemed of the LORD tell their story—those He redeemed from the hand of the foe, those He gathered from the lands
Isaiah 62:12 - They will be called the Holy People, the Redeemed of the LORD
to: 1 Peter 1:18-19 - You were redeemed not with perishable things like silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ
Revelation 5:9 - You are worthy because You were slain, and with Your blood You purchased for God persons from every tribe and language
9.context: The 'redeemed' who walk this highway are those purchased by God. In the Old Testament, redemption meant buying back from slavery; in the New Testament, Christ's blood is the purchase price. The redeemed of Isaiah become the blood-bought people of Revelation.

from: Isaiah 51:11 - Those the LORD has rescued will return. They will enter Zion with singing; everlasting joy will crown their heads
Psalm 126:5-6 - Those who sow with tears will reap with songs of joy; those who go out weeping will return with songs of joy
to: Revelation 21:4 - He will wipe every tear from their eyes. There will be no more death or mourning or crying or pain
1 Thessalonians 4:17 - We will be with the Lord forever. Therefore encourage one another with these words
10.context: Isaiah 35:10 and 51:11 are virtually identical—the promise is so important it's repeated. Sorrow fleeing away finds its ultimate fulfillment in Revelation 21 where death, mourning, crying, and pain are abolished. The ransomed return is both historical (from Babylon) and eschatological (to the new Jerusalem).

theme: God transforms the wilderness into paradise, heals every affliction, and builds a Highway of Holiness for the ransomed to return to Zion with everlasting joy

1.name: The Desert Blooms
1.transition: The wilderness rejoices and blossoms like a crocus, revealing the glory of the LORD

2.name: Strengthen the Weak
2.transition: Strengthen feeble hands, steady weak knees, and say to fearful hearts: your God will come

3.name: Healing for All
3.transition: The blind see, the deaf hear, the lame leap, the mute shout—water breaks forth in the wilderness

4.name: The Highway of Holiness
4.transition: A sacred highway for the redeemed—the ransomed return to Zion with everlasting joy

hinge.explanation: Promise Anchor (v4) — 'Your God will come' — the declaration that grounds every healing and transformation in the chapter

climax.explanation: Chapter Climax (v10) — The ransomed of the LORD return with singing; everlasting joy crowns their heads, and sorrow flees away
